pathophysiology_summary: Recurrent aphthous stomatitis is a common immune-mediated ulceration of non-keratinised oral mucosa (buccal, labial, ventral tongue, floor of mouth), presenting as painful round/oval ulcers with a yellow-grey pseudomembranous centre and erythematous halo, often triggered by stress, trauma or haematinic deficiency. Minor aphthae are <10mm and heal within 7–14 days without scarring. It is benign; the ground-truth task is (1) distinguishing it from herpetic (HSV) stomatitis (vesicular prodrome, keratinised gingiva/hard-palate involvement, systemic features) and other mimics, (2) managing pain to preserve oral intake and prevent dehydration (relevant here, living alone with no intake), and (3) safety-netting the two flags that matter: a single ulcer persisting >3 weeks (malignancy — needs biopsy) and dehydration.

differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent painful round ulcers with yellow-grey centre/red halo on non-keratinised mucosa, stress-triggered.
Screen for vesicular prodrome and keratinised gingiva/hard-palate involvement — absent here, argues against.
Wipeable white plaques, not discrete ulcers with halos — argues against.
Any single ulcer >3 weeks needs biopsy; recurrent self-healing pattern argues against here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Aphthous stomatitis leads; HSV excluded by mucosa distribution/no vesicles; persistent-ulcer malignancy safety-net noted.

differential.evidence_basis: Non-keratinised distribution, no vesicular prodrome, no systemic features, recurrent self-healing <3 weeks — classic minor aphthae; dehydration is the active issue.
Recurrent, self-healing, multiple small ulcers; escalation target only if a single ulcer persists >3 weeks.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative red-flag screen, minor aphthous stomatitis leads; topical therapy + analgesia-enabled hydration.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting; haematinic work-up (B12/folate/iron) via GP if frequently recurrent.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign recurrent minor aphthae.

red_flags.description: Dehydration — no oral intake, reduced urine output, postural lightheadedness (living alone)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pain-driven cessation of intake can cause dehydration requiring ED/IV fluids.

red_flags.description: Single ulcer persisting >3 weeks, or systemic features (fever, lymphadenopathy, spread beyond mouth)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Persistent ulcer requires biopsy to exclude malignancy; systemic features suggest infective/systemic cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed oral malignancy or systemic illness.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Mislabelling as herpes and prescribing antivirals
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Confusing aphthae with HSV; aphthous ulcers spare keratinised gingiva/palate and have no vesicular prodrome — antivirals are not indicated.
Focusing on the ulcers and missing that a patient living alone has stopped drinking.
Not safety-netting that a single non-healing ulcer needs biopsy.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Full intraoral examination for ulcer distribution/keratinised involvement
Assessment of mucous-membrane moisture / hydration status
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical analgesia/corticosteroid + analgesia-enabled hydration; ED if unable to rehydrate or dehydration signs; GP for haematinics and any persistent ulcer.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating HSV/malignancy as aphthae; under-recognising dehydration.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Minor aphthous ulcers: topical corticosteroid (e.g. triamcinolone dental paste) and topical analgesics; maintain hydration; investigate haematinics if frequently recurrent; biopsy any ulcer persisting beyond 3 weeks.
